# Material and Methods

The following methods and data were used in this project. AI tools were used to support text refinement and to assist with more advanced coding tasks.

## Data

This project uses two primary mobility data sources, tracked from two individuals: Google Timeline data and GPS Logger data. 
Google Timeline provides sparse but semantically enriched location records, including latitude/longitude coordinates, timestamps, and partially pre-classified activity labels (e.g., walking, driving, cycling). However, its temporal resolution is low and irregular, which limits fine-grained movement analysis. The Coordinates are not as precise as GPS data and can deviate several meters from the known location.
Over a tracking period of 17 days (08.04.2026 to 24.04.2026), a total of 5'420 points were recorded, of which 658 points were attributable to movement-related activity.

In contrast, GPS Logger data offers high-frequency, continuous tracking at fixed time intervals, containing raw coordinates and timestamps. 
This allows for detailed trajectory reconstruction but introduces noise, tracking holes (crashing app) and requires preprocessing.



## Used R packages

The project is implemented in R @RProject, @RStudio using a combination of data wrangling, time-series handling, spatial analysis, and cartographic visualization packages. The tidyverse and lubridate packages get used for cleaning, restructuring, filtering, summarising the movement data and management of time data. 
Spatial processing is mainly carried out with the sf package. OpenStreetMap road network data is accessed using osmdata and linked to the mobility trajectories through nearest-feature matching.
For visualisation, ggplot2 gets used for statistical plots and the package tmap and leaflet will be used to display the movement data on maps. 
Other packages used for processing or plotting include; jsonlite, dplyr, purrr, stringr, osmextract, data.table.


## Pre processing

### google timeline data

- Importing Google Timeline JSON data, extracting coordinates, timestamps and convert them, as well as delete errors.
- Calculating distances and time differences between two consecutive points. From these, the moving window is computed as the mean of both neighboring points.
- Detecting stationary and moving phases using distance-based thresholds (< 100 m) and segment trajectories.
- Estimating relevant locations such as home and ZHAW with most frequent coordinates in certain time frames.



### GPS data:



## Parameter calculation 

- Preparing the use of OpenStreetMap and its categories which includes loading the Swiss road/rail network lines, filtering to relevant categories, grouping these categories and match the points to the nearest feature. 
- Calculating movement parameters:
 * Simple calculations for speed, time out of home, travel distances and travel time to ZHAW
 * Road type as time share per road type group
- Visualising trajectories and movement patterns using maps and plots.


## Validation

The validation of the different calculated parameters and the road type matching with OpenStreetMap is based on methods described by @validation. The used methods include visualization and event validity. As the tracking data was collected by the authors, they do have knowledge of the correct events and can assign errors and estimate miscalculated values.
